title: Memory Management

primary_when: request is specifically about memory ownership, pooling, or allocation behavior
request is about reducing GC pressure or defining lifetime rules
must_not_own_when: request is mainly about UI layout, runtime patching, loader bootstrap, or metadata parsing

solves: controlling lifetimes of hot-path objects and buffers
reducing allocator and GC overhead where it matters
does_not_solve: performance issues caused by poor algorithms or layout alone
pooling by default in cold code without evidence
ownership ambiguity hidden behind convenience abstractions

core_principles: Make ownership and release responsibility explicit.
Reduce transient allocations in hot paths before reaching for broad pooling.
Pool only when lifecycle and capacity behavior are bounded and inspectable.
Prefer stable reusable buffers over repeated small temporary allocations.
Keep long-lived, medium-lived, and per-frame or per-pass data clearly separated.
constraints: Do not introduce pools that conceal leaks, stale state, or unbounded growth.
Do not retain memory indefinitely without a clear shrink or reset policy.
Do not optimize away allocations in cold paths while ignoring dominant hot costs.
Any reuse strategy must define reset rules and ownership transfer rules.
decision_rules: If a hot path repeatedly allocates the same shape of data, first test stable buffer reuse.
If objects cross subsystem boundaries, define ownership and disposal rules before optimizing allocation sites.
If pooled instances require heavy reset logic, question whether pooling is still a net win.
If temporary memory can be scoped to a pass or stage, prefer pass-local arenas or explicit reusable buffers.
If allocation pressure is low and the code is cold, avoid premature pooling complexity.

preferred_patterns: reusable buffers with clear reset points
bounded pools for stable hot-path object shapes
pass-scoped working memory separated from long-lived state
avoid_patterns: global pools with unclear return discipline
pooling as a blanket default across the whole system
shared mutable pooled state crossing unrelated domains
allocating formatting, wrapper, or adapter objects inside hot loops

recipes.name: Reusable Working Buffer
recipes.when: A system repeatedly builds temporary collections during each update or processing pass.
steps: Replace per-pass allocations with a reusable buffer owned by the system or pass host.
Define explicit clear or reset timing.
Verify that retained capacity remains bounded.

recipes.name: Bounded Object Pool
recipes.when: A stable hot-path object shape is created and retired frequently under controlled lifecycle rules.
steps: Define the owner responsible for acquisition and release.
Define reset semantics for reused instances.
Cap pool growth or define shrink behavior.
Validate that the pool removes meaningful hot-path cost instead of shifting it elsewhere.

pitfalls: Adding reuse without explicit reset and ownership semantics.
Retaining oversized buffers forever after short spikes.
Chasing allocation counts while ignoring bigger CPU or cache costs.
review_checklist: Is ownership explicit for allocated and reused memory?
Are hot-path temporary allocations either removed or justified?
Do pools or reusable buffers have reset and capacity discipline?
Are long-lived and short-lived allocations separated conceptually and operationally?
Was the optimization chosen based on measured pressure rather than habit?

references.title: Memory Management
references.interpretation: Use for allocation policy, pooling boundaries, and lifetime review.

references.title: Performance Domain Foundations
references.interpretation: Use for the larger cost model and measurement-first optimization framing.
